coredata.dc:title: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles capped with graphene quantum dots as multifunctional drug carriers for photo-thermal and redox-responsive release
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yan
Zhong, Shuangling
Xu, Lifeng
He, Shihao
Dou, Yueming
Zhao, Shengnan
Chen, Peng
Cui, Xuejun
coredata.dc:description: A novel photo-thermal and redox-responsive drug delivery carrier was developed by capping mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The disulfide bonds were introduced by amidation reaction between cystine and amino functionalized MSNs. Rhodamine B (RhB), a red fluorescent dye, was loaded into the mesopores of MSNs as the model drug and GQDs capped on MSNs as gatekeepers could prevent the release of RhB. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), nitrogen adsorption and desorption analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric (TG) analysis and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) proved that the nanocomposites MSNs capped with GODs were achieved successfully. The nanocomposites with the size of about 100 nm have excellent photo-thermal property originated from GQDs. Moreover, the nanocomposites were endowed with remarkable redox-responsion to glutathione (GSH) from disulfide bonds, and hence the loaded drugs could release controlably. In this work, we provided an exploration of photo-thermal and redox-responsive drug delivery system and the results proved that this drug delivery system can be considered as a promising candidate for drug delivery and stimuli-responsive release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Gatekeeper
Redox-responsive
Photo-thermal
Drug delivery system

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of MSNs (A) and MSNsSSGQDs (B); Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms (C) and the pore size distribution (D) of MSNs, MSNsSSHN2 and MSNsSSGQDs.
Small-angle X-ray diffraction patterns of MSNs (a), MSNsSSNH2 (b) and MSNsSSGQDs (c).
Size distributions of MSNs (a), MSNs-NH2 (b), MSNsSSNH2 (c) and MSNsSSGQDs (d).
FTIR spectrum of pure MSNs (a), MSNs-NH2 (b), MSNsSSNH2 (c) and MSNsSSGQDs (d) in A; TG analysis curves of pure MSNs (a), MSNs-NH2 (b), MSNsSSNH2 (c) and MSNsSSGQDs (d) in B.

(A) Photothermal heating curves of MSNsSSGQDs solutions at different concentrations: (a) 5 mg/ml, (b) 2 mg/ml, (c) 1 mg/ml and (d) 0 mg/ml under NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 2.5 W/cm2). (B) Releasing profiles of RhB from the RhB -loaded MSNsSSGQDs in PBS of pH = 7.4 at 37 °C, irradiated with/without 2.5 W/cm2 NIR light for 5 min at the indicated time points. Error bars indicate the standard error of the mean for N = 3 independent experiments.
Cumulative release of RhB from MSNsSSGQDs (a) and MSNsSSNH2 (b) in A; Cumulative release of RhB from MSNsSSGQDs without GSH (a), with 10 μM GSH (b) and with 10 mM GSH (c) in B.
The CLSM images of HeLa cells incubated with RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs. The images were obtained under magnification of 80.
Schematic illustration of drug loading and release.
Synthesis schematics of MSNs-NH2, MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs.
The property characterization of MSNs, MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs.
The mean diameter and average zeta potential of MSNs, MSNs-NH2, MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs in DI water.

head.ce:title: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles capped with graphene quantum dots as multifunctional drug carriers for photo-thermal and redox-responsive release

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Jilin Agricultural University, Changchun, 130118, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 70 Nanyang Drive, Singapore, 637457, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel photo-thermal and redox-responsive drug delivery carrier was developed by capping mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The disulfide bonds were introduced by amidation reaction between cystine and amino functionalized MSNs. Rhodamine B (RhB), a red fluorescent dye, was loaded into the mesopores of MSNs as the model drug and GQDs capped on MSNs as gatekeepers could prevent the release of RhB. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), nitrogen adsorption and desorption analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric (TG) analysis and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) proved that the nanocomposites MSNs capped with GODs were achieved successfully. The nanocomposites with the size of about 100 nm have excellent photo-thermal property originated from GQDs. Moreover, the nanocomposites were endowed with remarkable redox-responsion to glutathione (GSH) from disulfide bonds, and hence the loaded drugs could release controlably. In this work, we provided an exploration of photo-thermal and redox-responsive drug delivery system and the results proved that this drug delivery system can be considered as a promising candidate for drug delivery and stimuli-responsive release.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, cancer is still regarded as one of the most common cause of human death and the chemotherapy is still one of the most adopted methods to treat cancer [1–4]. However, the chemotherapy might induce systemic side-effects because the traditional chemotherapy agents could not distinguish between the normal and cancerous cells [5–7]. In the past decades, a great deal of studies have focused on controlled drug delivery system (DDS) since they can increase the therapeutic efficiency by reducing systemic toxicity and undesired side effects. As we know, the ideal controlled DDS should deliver drugs without any leakage during drug transportation and release drugs rapidly once reaching the targeted tissues or cells [8]. Therefore, it is very necessary to design the DDS that could deliver drugs into diseased tissue without any unwanted premature leakage for effective releasing of drugs at the target site [9–11].

Among the proposed DDS, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) have gained increasing attentions as a perfect drug delivery carrier owing to its excellent biocompatibility, homogeneous controllable particle size, large pore volume, high specific surface area and easy surface modification, which can be further grafted with the gatekeepers to cover the pore entrance [12–20]. And some kinetic models for drug release from functionalized mesoporous-silica supports research were build-up and could be used for helping us designing the controlled release system [21–24]. Up to now, the MSNs-based controlled DDS have been constructed by end-capping with a series of gratekeepers, including polymers, organic molecules, biomacromolecules and inorganic nanoparticles [25–28]. As a novel fluorescence materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) had been widely applied in biomedical field [29], including drug delivery, cell imaging and DNA detection originated from its excellent biocompatibility, low cell toxicity, strong NIR absorbance, high photo-thermal conversion efficiency, excellent thermal conductivity and small lateral size [30]. A large number of research results indicate that GQDs can effectively convert NIR light energy into heat, potentially acting as a photo-thermal agent [4,31]. Also, GQDs could be employed as gatekeepers to cover MSNs and the resulted MSNs-based DDS could release the loaded drugs at the expected site in response to diverse stimuli, such as pH [32], temperature [33], photoirradiation [34], redox potential [35,36] and enzyme responsiveness [37]. Fu and his co-workers reported a new drug delivery system using GQDs to cap onto the nanopores of MSNs through an acid-cleavable acetal bond [38]. Li et al. developed a novel drug nanocarrier based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer by capping GQDs onto mesoporous silica nanoparticles via ATP aptamer for real-time monitoring of ATP-triggered drug release [39]. Among the various stimuli used for DDS, redox potential has attracted much attentions due to the remarkable difference of glutathione (GSH) concentration between the extracellular (2–10 μM) and intracellular (10 mM) environment of the cancer cells. Generally, the concentration of GSH in the cancer cells is significantly higher than that in the normal cells [40]. Since disulfide bonds can be broken by GSH, redox potential based on the disulfide bonds is expected as an ideal stimulus to achieve controlling drug release. However, there is few report on the MSNs-based drug delivery system with GQDs as gatekeeper and GSH responsive.

In the present work, we design a facile and effective method to prepare a smart MSNs-based drug delivery system by capping functionalized MSNs with GQDs (Scheme 1 ). MSNs first were modified with APTES to obtain aminated MSNs (MSNs-NH2), which could be further reacted with cystine to achieve aminated MSNs with disulfide bonds (MSNsSSNH2) nanoparticles. Furthermore, the drugs could be loaded into the porous of MSNsSSNH2 under stirring and GQDs were capped on MSNs to prevent the release of drugs by combining GQDs with MSNsSSNH2 nanoparticles through the amidation reaction. The MSNs capped with GQDs (MSNsSSGQDs) nanocomposites would be endowed with photo-thermal property from GQDs. Eventually, the disulfide bonds would be broken in the presence of a certain concentration of GSH and the drug could release rapidly from the mesoporous channels of MSNs [30]. In addition, the photo-thermal and redox-responsive DDS has an excellent capping efficiency and the cumulative release of drug was also investigated. The reductive-responsive release drug delivery systems MSNsSSGQDs could precisely kill tumors cell, significantly improve the drug utilization and reduce the severe side effects on healthy tissues. Therefore, MSNsSSGQDs could be used as anti-cancer drugs carriers for the cancer therapy or fluorescent tracer carriers for the cancer imaging in biomedical application.

N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminepropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry, Co. (Shanghai, China). Nitric acid (HNO3), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Rhodamine B (RhB, Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Company, Guangzhou, China). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, Huishi Bio-chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, Shanghai, China). Cystine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemical regents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without any further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by us. The water used in all experiments was prepared in a three-stage Millipore Milli-Q Plus 185 purification system and had a resistivity higher than 18.2 MΩ cm.

GQDs were prepared from carbon black (Vulcan CX-72, Cabot Corporation) by refluxed with nitric acid according to our previous report [41,42]. The procedure is described as follows: Under the ceaseless stirring, dried carbon black (0.2 g) was added into HNO3 solution (50 ml, 6 M) followed by refluxing for 24h. The resulting solution was centrifuged (2770 g) for 10min to obtain a supernatant. After evaporating the redundant nitric acid and water in the supernatant, a reddish-brown powder was obtained. The dried powder was dispersed in DI water and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The obtained solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 48 h. At last, the GQDs were obtained by freeze drying.

MSNs were prepared as previously reported in literature [36,43]. First NaOH solution (3.5 ml, 2 M) was introduced to CTAB solution (1 g CTAB was dissolved in deionized water 480 ml) under vigorous agitation at 80 °C. Then, TEOS (5 ml) was added dropwise into the above solution over 10 min and the mixture was stirred vigorously for 2 h to obtain a white precipitation. The resulting nanoparticles were collected by repeated centrifugation (9000 rpm, 20 min) and washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times. The obtained solid was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h and calcinated at 550 °C for 6 h to remove the templating surfactant for fabricating the pores.

The surface of MSNs was modified by APTES to obtain MSNs-NH2 as shown in Scheme 2 A. MSNs (0.2 g), acetic acid (200 μl) and APTES (50 μl) were added into 20 ml of ethonal and the mixture was stirred continually for 24 h at room temperature. After washing with ethanol, the resulting product (MSNs-NH2) was dried under reduced pressure for 12 h at 60 °C.

MSNsSSNH2 was obtained by an amidation reaction between the amino of MSNs-NH2 and the carboxyl groups of Cys (Scheme 2). First, Cys (10 mg) was dispersed in PBS (pH = 5.0) containing EDC (50 mg) and NHS (25 mg). After 1 h, the synthesized MSNs-NH2 (40 mg) was added to the above solution and the mixture was stirred for 24 h at the room temperature. The obtained MSNsSSNH2 particles were separated by centrifugation and washed with PBS to remove the excess Cys.

GQDs were covalently grafted onto the external surfaces of MSNs also by an amidation reaction between the amino of MSNsSSNH2 and the carboxyl groups of GQDs. In brief, the obtained GQDs (1 mg), EDC (10 mg) and NHS (5 mg) were dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water. The solution was stirred for 1 h to achieve carboxyl active for further connecting with MSNsSSNH2 particles. Then, MSNsSSNH2 particles (20 mg) were introduced into the above solution and the mixture kept stirring overnight. The resulting MSNsSSGQDs were collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and dried under vacuum.

Typically, MSNsSSNH2 particles (20 mg) were dispersed in 5 ml of deionized water. Then, 5 ml of RhB (1 mg/ml) solution as a mode hydrophilic drug was loaded into above solution under stirring for 24 h. The obtained solution was called RhB-loaded MSNsSSNH2. The GQDs, EDC and NHS were dispersed into deionized water and the mixture was stirred for 1 h. After activating GQDs, the RhB-loaded MSNsSSNH2 particles were added to the above solution, stirred for another 24 h, centrifuged and washed with deionized water. Finally, RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs were dried under vacuum. Besides that, the centrifugal liquid and washing water should be collected in order to confirm the amount of unloaded RhB.

To investigate the photo-thermal efficacy of MSNsSSGQDs, 1 ml of MSNsSSGQDs (0–5 mg/ml) aqueous solution was put into a quartz cuvette and exposed to an 808 nm NIR laser at 2.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. The temperature variations of solution were monitored with an infrared thermal camera (Magnity Electronics, China).

The heat from converting NIR light energy by GQDs could increase temperature of solution, which could accelerate the broken of disulfide bonds, and hence result in the improvement of the drug release rate. To determine the light-stimulated drug release kinetics, 1 ml of RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs solution (1 mg/ml) in a quartz cuvette was irradiated under 808 nm NIR laser at 2.5 W/cm2 for a certain time at different indicated time points. The solution was centrifugated to collect the released RhB and then was determined with UV–Vis spectrometer.

To monitor the redox-responsive drug release of MSNsSSGQDs, 6.0 ml of RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs suspension was put into a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight: 7000 Da) and then soaked into 50 ml PBS solution including GSH (10 μM or 10 mM) and vibrated under 150 rpm at 37 °C. The released RhB was analyzed with UV spectrometer. Furthermore, the release profile of RhB in the absence of GSH was also determined.

To research the cellular uptake of MSNsSSGQDs, Hela cells were seeded in twelve-well plates with 1.0 × 105 cells per well. The cells were incubated in serum-free medium in an atmosphere of 5% (v/v) CO2 in air at 37 °C for 24 h prior to cellular endocytosis. Subsequently, the cells were incubated for another 6 h in serum-free medium with RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs (1.0 ml). After harvesting and washing with PBS (pH 7.4), the cells were fixed with ethanol (75%). Finally, the fixed cells were investigated with confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM).

The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potentials of nanoparticles were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a 90Plus/BI-MAS and each sample was measured 10 times. The UV–vis absorption was observed by the UV-2550 spectrophotometer from Shimadzu (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was recorded on a Rigaku D/MAX 2550 diffractometer with Cu Kα source. Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometric (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet Instruments Research Series 5PC Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer using KBr powder pressed pellets. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image (SU8020, Tokyo, Japan) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, Tokyo, Japan) were performed to confirm the morphology and mesoporous structure of the MSNs. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed with a Pyris 1TGA (PerkinElmer) at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in the air atmosphere over the range of 100–800 °C. Nitrogen adsorption and desorption analysis was measured at 77 K using a surface area analyzer (V-Sorb2800P, China).

The structure and morphology of MSNs and MSNsSSGQDs were monitored by TEM. As displayed in the TEM images (Fig. 1 A), an array of well-ordered mesoporous network could be observed in the MSN nanoparticles, the mean diameter of the synthesized MSNs was about 100 nm with 2–4 nm of mesopore size. After capping, GQDs can be clearly observed on the surface of MSNs in Fig. 1 B. In addition, the surface area and average pore diameter of samples were measured by BET nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution analysis. As shown in Fig. 1C, the obtained nanoparticles can be classified as type IV isotherms characteristic of mesoporous materials. From Fig. 1C and D, the surface area (SBET) and cumulative pore volume (Vt) of pure MSNs were calculated to be 678.52 m2/g and 0.82 cm3/g, respectively. The average pore diameter (Dp) was about 2.8 nm as summarized in Table 1 , indicating that the MSNs could be used for the further functionalization without blocking the porous channels. After modification with Cys, the SBET and Vt of nanoparticles were decreased to 449.57 m2/g and 0.49 cm3/g. Moreover, after capping with GQDs, the SBET and Vt of MSNsSSGQDs were reduced obviously (209.97 m2/g and 0.29 cm3/g), indicating that GQDs were capped on the surface of the functionalized MSNs successfully.

Fig. 2 shows the small-angle XRD patterns of pure MSNs, MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs. The XRD diffraction peaks at 2.54, 4.32 and 5.02 could be indexed to (100), (110) and (200) diffraction peaks corresponded to the typical pattern of ordered 2D hexagonal (P6mm) symmetry [44,45]. After modifying with Cys and GQDs, the intensities of the three well-resolved peaks were decreased gradually, suggesting that the reduction in mesoporous ordering. However, the modified materials still maintain their well-ordered mesostructure after two-step modification.

The hydrodynamic diameter and size distribution of the samples were measured by DLS. As shown in Fig. 3 and Table 2 , the sizes of samples were increased gradually with step-by-step modification. The average hydrodynamic diameter of MSNs, MSNs-NH2, MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs were 122.5 nm, 136.8 nm, 143.2 nm and 158.9 nm, respectively. The sizes of MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs were larger than that of MSNs due to the modification of Cys and capping of GQDs. The size distribution of the obtained MSNsSSGQDs was about 120–200 nm. Clearly, the hydrodynamic diameter of MSNs was larger than that obtained from TEM because of the different principles between the two characterization methods. Generally, the size of nanoparticles from TEM image is achieved in a dried state while the DLS gives the hydrodynamic diameter [45,46].

The zeta potential characterization was also employed to confirm the modification of MSNs successfully. As shown in Table 2, the zeta potential of pure MSNs was a negative value of −26.31 mV due to the hydroxyl groups on the surface. After modifying with APTES, the zeta potential increased to 15.8 mV due to the increment of amido groups on the surface. In addition, the zeta potential of MSNsSSNH2 further increased to 25.07 mV due to the grafting of Cys. After MSNsSSNH2 reacting with GQDs (the zeta potential of GQDs is −3.68 mV), the zeta potential of MSNsSSGQDs decreased to 6.05 mV owing to the amidation reaction between amido groups of MSNsSSNH2 and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs. The change of zeta potential also could verify that the MSNsSSGQDs have been obtained successfully.

FTIR spectroscopy was also used to analyse the samples. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the characteristic absorption peaks of pure MSNs at 3432 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1were attributed to vibrational mode of SiOH and antisymmetric stretching vibration of SiOSi, respectively. The vibrational peak at 1556 cm−1 was observed in the MSNs-NH2 spectrum, which was assigned to the bending vibration of NH. The characteristic absorption peaks of MSNsSSNH2 at 1634 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1 were attributed to the flexural vibration absorption of Amide I and Amide II, proving that MSNs-NH2 has been modified by Cys successfully.

The TG analysis curves of MSNs, MSNs-NH2, MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs are illustrated in Fig. 4 B. When the temperature increased from 100 °C to 800 °C, the weight loss of pure MSNs, MSNs-NH2, MSNsSSNH2 and MSNsSSGQDs were 1.15%, 6.5%, 8.7% and 10.78%, respectively. Compared with MSNs-NH2, the increment of weight loss of MSNsSSNH2 further confirmed that the MSNs-NH2 was modified by Cys successfully. In addition, the degree of degradation of MSNsSSGQDs increased by 2.08% compared with MSNsSSNH2, owing to the weight loss of GQDs capping on the surface of MSNs.

In order to confirm the loading capacity and drug release ability, the RhB as a hydrophilic drug model was loaded into the MSNsSSGQDs nanoparticles. The amount of unloaded drug was determined from the maximum absorbance (λ = 558 nm) according to a standard curve in the same buffer. The drug loading efficiency (DLE) was defined as follows: DLE = (mass of drug loaded in MSNsSSGQDs/mass of drug loaded MSNsSSGQDs) × 100%. The drug encapsulation efficiency (DEE) was defined as follows: DEE = (mass of drug loaded in MSNsSSGQDs/mass of feed drug) × 100% [35]. The result shown that the DLE of RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs was 8.2% and the DEE was 36%.

The photo-thermal performance of GQDs and GQDs-mesoporous materials have been reported recently [47–49]. To determine the photo-thermal performance of MSNsSSGQDs, the solutions with appropriate concentrations of MSNsSSGQDs were irradiated with NIR laser (808 nm, 2.5 W/cm2). As shown in Fig. 5 (A) Fig. 5 A, the temperature of MSNsSSGQDs solutions increased gradually with the prolonging of irradiation time. After NIR irradiation for 10 min, the temperature of MSNsSSGQDs solutions under concentrations of 1, 2 and 5 mg/ml raised 12.2, 20.3 and 30.8 °C, respectively. On the contrary, the temperature of water elevated less than 3 °C after irradiation for 10 min, indicating that MSNsSSGQDs could be a potential candidate applied in the field of tumor photo-thermal therapy. Moreover, the photo-thermal performance of MSNsSSGQDs would influent on their drug release, too. It could be seen obviously from Fig. 5 B that the drug release increased remarkably when the MSNsSSGQDs solution (1 mg/ml) was exposed to a NIR laser (808 nm, 2.5 W/cm2) for 5 min interval of each 4 h.

To verify the redox-responsive ability of the MSNsSSGQDs nanoparticles, in vitro release studies were conducted in pH 7.4 PBS at 37 °C. The amount release of RhB could be measured by UV–vis spectroscopy from the absorbance at 558 nm according to a standard curve of RhB in the same buffer. The release experiments of RhB-loaded MSNsSSNH2 and RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs were firstly performed. From Fig. 6 A, it was found that little amounts of RhB were released from MSNsSSGQDs to the solution and the cumulative release of RhB from MSNsSSNH2 was as high as 90% in 12 h, signifying that MSNsSSNH2 nanoparticles have been capped by GQDs successfully and the GQDs-capped MSNs drug delivery system exhibited an excellent capping efficiency. Moreover, the RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs were treated with 10 μM GSH and 10 mM GSH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6B, when there was no GSH in the solution, the cumulative release of RhB increased slowly with the release time. When the concentration of GSH was 10 μM, approximately 20% of RhB was released over 24 h. However, in the presence of 10 mM GSH, the cumulative release of RhB could reach about 40% at 5 h and about 70% of the total RhB at 24 h because the capped GQDs were removed with the cleavage of disulfide bonds under the reductivity of GSH. Obviously, the RhB cumulative release percentage increased with an increasing concentration of GSH. This result suggested that the MSNsSSGQDs, as an effective drug carrier, could prevent the release of drug in the delivery process and possessed an excellent redox-responsive ability to control drugs release.

In order to investigate the cellular uptake of MSNsSSGQDs, the Hela cells were incubated with RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs in the serum-free medium and investigated by CLSM. It could be observed clearly from Fig. 7 that there was red fluorescence in the intracellular regions of Hela cells after incubation for 6 h, indicating that the RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs could be swallowed by cells. Therefore, it can be concluded that the RhB-loaded MSNsSSGQDs could be taken in easily by cells and would be promising potential for application as drug carriers.

In this work, a biocompatible photo-thermal and redox-responsive DDS have been designed and constructed by capping GQDs on the surface of the functionalized MSNs. The dye, RhB, as a model drug, was encapsulated into the pores of MSNs and then capped by the GQDs. The in vitro release demonstrated that the RhB could be entrapped with little leakage in the absence of GSH, which could protect the drugs during delivery. However, the release of RhB could be controlled under NIR laser irradiation and in the presence of moderate GSH. The release of RhB dramatically increased owing to the cleavage of the disulfide bond with the addition of appropriate GSH. This work provided an exploration about MSNs-based stimuli-responsive DDS and the results proved that MSNsSSGQDs could be considered as a promising and smart drug carrier for photo-thermal and redox-responsive drugs controlled release.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Natural Science Foundation of the Science and Technology Department of Jilin Province, PR China (No. 20180101072JC), the Key Program of the Science and Technology Department of Jilin Province, PR China (No. 20180201082SF) and the scientific research Program of the Education Department of Jilin Province (P. R. China) during the 13th Five-Year Plan Period (No. JJKH20180119KJ). Dedicated to Professor Helmuth Möhwald.
